EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 18 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added `firstInsuranceSurcharge` inline in `quote`. Let me evaluate naming and structure.

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `firstInsuranceSurcharge` — clear, reveals intent (the surcharge for a first-time policy). Good.
- `quote` — clearly the public entry point. Good.
- `applyLoyaltyDiscount` — descriptive, returns adjusted premium. Good.
- `basePremium`, `policyBase`, `groups` — all clear.

Decision: Keep all current names. They reveal intent well.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass (focus on the changed `quote` function)

```
export function quote(input: QuoteInput): QuoteResult {
  const groups = [...groupByType(input.items).values()];        // binding + invocation + invocation(spread/values)
  const basePremium = groups.reduce(...);                       // binding + invocation(reduce) + invocation(basePremiumForGroup) + binding(sum,group)
  const firstInsuranceSurcharge = input.firstInsurance ? basePremium * 0.1 : 0;  // binding + conditional + invocation(*) + constant(0.1) + constant(0)
  const policyBase = applyLoyaltyDiscount(basePremium, input.yearsWithMHPCO);    // binding + invocation
  return { premium: policyBase + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE };     // invocation(+) x2
}
```

Approximate mass of `quote` body: bindings(4: groups, basePremium, firstInsuranceSurcharge, policyBase) + several invocations + 1 conditional + 2 constants ≈ **~30**.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 18 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: There is one concern. The `firstInsuranceSurcharge` uses an inline magic literal `0.1` directly, while every other rate in the file is a named constant (`CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`, etc.). This is an inconsistency that hurts intent-revelation and is exactly the kind of named-constant treatment the rest of the file already establishes.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication introduced.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Extracting the constant adds one binding but matches existing pattern; net effect is consistency, not over-engineering. I will not extract a helper function (only one test exercises this; a helper would anticipate nothing but adds unneeded elements for a single-line expression).

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

Extract the magic `0.1` into a named constant, consistent with the file's existing convention. This does not anticipate any future test — it only renames a literal already in use by the passing test.

content.text:
All 18 tests still pass. The refactoring is complete.

## Step 6: Mass Change

The change converts the inline literal `0.1` (constant, mass 1) into a named binding reference (`FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, also mass 1 at the use site) plus one module-level constant declaration. Functional mass of `quote` is essentially unchanged (~30 → ~30). The win is Rule 2 (clarity/consistency), which trumps APP.

## Step 7: Report

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:

**Naming Evaluation**: `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `quote`, `applyLoyaltyDiscount`, and all bindings already reveal intent — no renames needed.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted the magic literal `0.1` into a named constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, matching the file's established convention where every other rate (`CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`) is named. This removed an inconsistency the Green phase introduced.

**No anticipation**: I did not extract a `applyFirstInsuranceSurcharge` helper or generalize the surcharge logic — only one test exercises this behavior, and a helper would add elements (Rule 4) without any current test demanding it. The change touches only the literal already used by the passing test.

**Mass Change**: ~30 → ~30 (functionally unchanged; named-constant substitution). Rule 2 clarity gain prioritized over APP per the rules.

**Tests**: 18 passing, all green.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test (`second quote in scenario → 15% follow-up discount applies policy-wide`).
